title: Linux Build & Toolchain
description: Every engineer compiles code — but understanding the toolchain (compiler, linker, loader, libraries, build systems) separates those who debug build issues in minutes from those who fight them for hours. At FAANG, build toolchain knowledge is essential for: debugging linker errors, optimizing binary performance, reducing container image size, and working with hermetic build systems like Bazel.

sections.title: The C Compilation Pipeline
sections.description: C/C++ compilation is a multi-stage pipeline: preprocessing (handle #include, #define, #ifdef), compilation (translate to assembly), assembly (translate to machine code/object file), linking (combine object files + libraries into executable). Understanding each stage helps you debug build errors and optimize build times.
sections.codeBlock:
# === The four stages ===
# Stage 1: Preprocessing
gcc -E hello.c -o hello.i             # Preprocessed output (expanded macros, includes)

# Stage 2: Compilation
gcc -S hello.i -o hello.s             # Assembly output (human-readable)

# Stage 3: Assembly
gcc -c hello.s -o hello.o             # Object file (machine code, not executable)

# Stage 4: Linking
gcc hello.o -o hello                  # Executable (linked with libc, crt0, etc.)

# Or all at once:
gcc -Wall -O2 -o hello hello.c

# === Object file inspection ===
file hello.o                          # ELF 64-bit LSB relocatable, x86-64
nm hello.o                            # Symbol table (functions, globals)
nm -C hello.o                         # Demangled C++ names
objdump -d hello.o                    # Disassembly
objdump -t hello.o                    # Symbol table
objdump -x hello.o                    # All headers, sections, symbols

# === Shared library dependencies ===
ldd /usr/bin/python3                  # List shared library dependencies
# linux-vdso.so.1 — Virtual Dynamic Shared Object (kernel-provided)
# libpython3.10.so.1.0 — Python runtime
# libc.so.6 — GNU C Library (glibc)
# /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 — Dynamic linker/loader

# === ELF sections ===
readelf -S hello.o                    # List sections (.text, .data, .bss, .rodata)
readelf -h hello.o                    # ELF header (architecture, entry point, flags)
readelf -l hello.o                    # Program headers (segments for loading)
# .text — executable code
# .data — initialized global/static variables
# .bss — uninitialized global variables (zero-initialized)
# .rodata — read-only data (string literals, const)
# .symtab — symbol table

# === Strip debugging symbols ===
strip hello                           # Remove symbols (smaller binary, harder to debug)
strip --strip-debug hello             # Keep global symbols, remove debug info
# FAANG production: binaries are stripped, debug symbols are archived separately

sections.title: Shared Libraries: SONAME, RPATH & LD_LIBRARY_PATH
sections.description: Shared libraries (.so files) are loaded at runtime by the dynamic linker (ld-linux.so). The linker searches: RPATH (hardcoded in binary) → LD_LIBRARY_PATH → /etc/ld.so.cache → /lib, /usr/lib. Mismatched library versions cause the infamous 'symbol not found' errors. At FAANG, RPATH is preferred over LD_LIBRARY_PATH for production — it's baked into the binary and can't be accidentally overridden.
sections.codeBlock:
# === SONAME (Shared Object Name) ===
# Libraries have three names:
# 1. Real name: libcrypto.so.3.0.0 (full version)
# 2. SONAME: libcrypto.so.3 (compatible ABI version)
# 3. Linker name: libcrypto.so (no version, for -lcrypto flag)

# Check SONAME:
objdump -p /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcrypto.so.3 | grep SONAME
# SONAME is embedded in the library file
# ldconfig creates symlinks from SONAME to real name

# === RPATH vs RUNPATH ===
# RPATH: search path embedded in binary, searched BEFORE LD_LIBRARY_PATH
# RUNPATH: similar but searched AFTER LD_LIBRARY_PATH
# Prefer RUNPATH (allows overriding with LD_LIBRARY_PATH for debugging)

gcc -Wl,-rpath,'$ORIGIN/../lib' -o myapp myapp.o
# $ORIGIN expands to the directory containing the executable
# This is how portable app bundles work (e.g., AppImage)

# Check RPATH:
readelf -d myapp | grep -E "(RPATH|RUNPATH)"

# === LD_LIBRARY_PATH (use for development, not production) ===
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/opt/custom/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
./myapp                                 # Searches custom path first

# === ldconfig — update library cache ===
sudo ldconfig                          # Rebuild /etc/ld.so.cache
sudo ldconfig -p | grep nvda          # Search cached libraries by name

# === ld.so.conf — system-wide library paths ===
cat /etc/ld.so.conf                    # Include /etc/ld.so.conf.d/*.conf
ls /etc/ld.so.conf.d/
# Each .conf file adds a search path

# === Library versioning best practices ===
# libfoo.so.1 -> libfoo.so.1.0.0 (major.minor.patch)
# Major version change = ABI break
# Minor/patch = backward-compatible
# Never replace a library with incompatible ABI without changing SONAME

# === Static vs shared libraries ===
# Static (.a): linked into executable, larger binary, no runtime deps
# Shared (.so): loaded at runtime, smaller binary, memory sharing
# FAANG production: mostly shared (container images share library pages)
# For small utilities: static (Go is fully static by default)

sections.title: Build Systems: Make, CMake, Bazel & Hermetic Builds
sections.description: At FAANG, build systems are hermetic — isolated from the developer's machine, producing identical outputs from identical inputs. Bazel (Google), Buck (Meta), and Pants (Twitter) enforce dependency tracking, remote caching, and incremental builds. Understanding build system concepts — dependency graph, caching, sandboxing, remote execution — is essential for infrastructure engineers.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Make ===
# Simple but limited: no dependency tracking, timestamp-based
# Every FAANG engineer should be able to read a Makefile

# === CMake ===
# Generates build files for different systems (Make, Ninja, Xcode, VS)
# CMakeLists.txt example:
# cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.20)
# project(myapp CXX)
# add_executable(myapp main.cpp util.cpp)
# target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE fmt)

cmake -B build -G Ninja .           # Configure (generate Ninja build files)
cmake --build build                 # Build

# === Bazel (hermetic build system — Google's standard) ===
# BUILD file:
# cc_binary(
#     name = "myapp",
#     srcs = ["main.cc", "util.cc"],
#     deps = [":mylib", "@fmt//:fmt"],
# )

bazel build //:myapp                 # Build (hermetic, cached, distributed)
bazel test //:tests                  # Run tests
bazel query 'deps(//:myapp)'         # Show dependency graph

# Hermetic build principles:
# 1. All inputs declared (source, tools, environment)
# 2. No network access during build
# 3. No access to /usr, /opt, etc.
# 4. Outputs depend only on inputs (no timestamps, no hostname)
# 5. Remote caching: rebuild is instant if inputs haven't changed

# === Ninja (fast incremental builds) ===
ninja -C build                       # Faster than make (parallel by default)

# === ccache (compiler cache) ===
export CCACHE_DIR=~/.ccache
export PATH=/usr/lib/ccache:$PATH    # Transparent compiler caching
# Second build of same file: instant (cache hit)

sections.title: LTO, PGO & Binary Optimization
sections.description: Modern compilers offer profile-guided and link-time optimizations that can improve performance by 10-30%. LTO (Link-Time Optimization) enables cross-module inlining and optimization. PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) uses runtime profiling data to guide optimization decisions. At FAANG, PGO is standard for critical services — the performance gain in compute-heavy workloads justifies the build complexity.
sections.codeBlock:
# === LTO (Link-Time Optimization) ===
# Traditional: compile each .c file separately, then link
# LTO: compile to intermediate representation (IR), optimize at link time

gcc -flto -O2 -o myapp file1.c file2.c   # LTO enabled
# Benefits: cross-module inlining, devirtualization, dead code elimination
# Cost: slower linking (~2x), more memory during link

# === PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) ===
# Step 1: Compile with instrumentation
gcc -fprofile-generate -O2 -o myapp.prof myapp.c

# Step 2: Run with representative workload
./myapp.prof < representative_input > /dev/null
# Generates .gcda files with profiling data

# Step 3: Recompile with profile data
gcc -fprofile-use -O2 -o myapp myapp.c
# Now: branch prediction, inlining, and loop optimization use real profiling data

# AutoFDO (Linux 5.x+): use perf data instead of instrumented binary
# perf record -b ./myapp  # Branch-recorded profiling
# create_gcov --profile=perf.data --gcov=myapp.gcov ./myapp
# gcc -fprofile-use=myapp.gcov -O2 -o myapp myapp.c

# === Size optimization ===
gcc -Os -o small_binary myapp.c          # Optimize for size (embedded, containers)
# Compare: -O2 (speed) vs -Os (size) — typical difference is 30-50%

# === Debug symbols ===
gcc -g -o myapp.debug myapp.c            # Full debug symbols
gcc -g1 -o myapp.debug myapp.c           # Minimal debug symbols (line numbers only)
objcopy --only-keep-debug myapp.debug myapp.dbg   # Extract debug info
objcopy --strip-debug myapp.debug myapp   # Strip for production
# FAANG pattern: separate debug symbols (myapp.dbg) uploaded to artifact store

# === Binary size analysis ===
size myapp                               # text, data, bss, total
bloaty myapp                             # Symbol-level size breakdown (install: apt install bloaty)
nm myapp | sort -k2,2 | uniq -c | sort -rn | head  # Largest symbol categories

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Your program compiles fine on your machine but fails with 'cannot open shared object file' on the server. Why?'

A: 'Missing shared library or wrong search path. Check with ldd myapp to find the missing library. Common fixes: (1) Install the library on the server (apt install). (2) Set LD_LIBRARY_PATH. (3) Link with RPATH/RUNPATH embedded in the binary. (4) The library version on the server is too old — check SONAME compatibility. At FAANG, this is solved by containers — the binary and all its shared libraries are in the container image, guaranteed to match.'

Q: 'What's the difference between dynamic and static linking, and when would you use each?'

A: 'Dynamic linking loads libraries at runtime — smaller binaries, memory sharing between processes, and library updates without recompiling. Downside: dependency hell (wrong library version = crash). Static linking embeds everything into the binary — no runtime dependencies, fully portable, but larger binaries and no library updates without rebuilding. At FAANG: most services use dynamic linking within containers (container = static deployment unit, libraries inside are fixed). Go uses fully static linking by default. For C/C++, static linking is preferred for small command-line tools and for Alpine-based containers (musl libc vs glibc compatibility issues).'

Q: 'How would you set up a build system for a monorepo with 1000 microservices?'

A: 'Use a hermetic build system like Bazel. Key requirements: (1) Explicit dependency declarations — every build target declares its deps. (2) Remote caching — builds reuse cached outputs from CI, not just local. (3) Remote execution — distribute build across hundreds of machines. (4) Incremental builds — changing one service rebuilds only that service and its dependents. (5) Sandboxed builds — no access to /usr, no network during build. (6) Multi-language support — Java, C++, Python, Go, Rust all in one repo. (7) CI integration — build and test only what changed (affected target detection). At FAANG, this scale of build system is a dedicated team's responsibility — the build infrastructure is as critical as the production infrastructure.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Linkers & Loaders — John Levine, the definitive reference on linking
  • Bazel Build System — Google's build system documentation
  • GCC Optimization Options — Official GCC optimization documentation

details:
FAANG Architect's Note: The build toolchain is one of the most underappreciated areas of systems knowledge. I've seen senior engineers stumble on basic linker errors, not understand why 'it works on my machine', or ship binaries without LTO/PGO that could have been 20% faster. At FAANG, the build system is a force multiplier: a well-optimized build pipeline means faster iteration cycles, smaller deployment artifacts, and better runtime performance. Every hour spent understanding the toolchain pays back tenfold in reduced debugging time and improved efficiency.

Production Tip: Always check ldd on your binary before deploying. A 'not found' library in production causes immediate crash on startup — one of the easiest bugs to catch in CI but one of the most common. Also: set -Wl,--as-needed in your LDFLAGS. This removes unnecessary library dependencies, reducing startup time and preventing ABI conflicts from unused libraries. In containers, always run ldd /app/myapp | grep 'not found' as a build step — if it passes, the container will start.
